topic: An artist's work is displayed without their permission
raw_output:
[story 1]

The gallery lights felt too bright, harsh against the delicate oils on the canvas. Elias stood near the entrance, his hands clenched so tightly his knuckles were white. Every brushstroke, every carefully rendered shadow, belonged to him—a private language he’d poured his very soul into. Now, it hung there, illuminated for strangers, a silent, unauthorized exhibit. He watched a young couple point, their murmurs echoing in the hushed space, and a deep, cold ache settled in his chest. He took a slow, measured breath, trying to steady the tremor in his legs. He wanted to walk in, to reclaim the quiet sanctity of his studio, but the sheer weight of the display kept him rooted to the spot, a prisoner of his own creation.

[story 2]

"It’s… breathtaking," a voice drifted over. I leaned against the cool marble pedestal, trying to appear composed, but my jaw was tight. The vibrant chaos of my latest series, the very essence of my recent struggles and triumphs, was being admired by people who hadn't even known my name. A tightness constricted my throat. I watched a woman trace the line of a particularly anguished face on the canvas, and a strange, hollow feeling washed over me. It wasn't anger that rose, just a profound sense of exposure, as if a vital part of myself had been carelessly laid bare for public consumption. I shifted my weight, the sound of my expensive shoes on the floor sounding unnervingly loud in the silence.

[story 3]

The air in the viewing room was thick with polite appraisal. I kept my gaze fixed on the floor, tracing the pattern of the polished wood, anything to avoid meeting anyone’s eyes. My charcoal sketches, my raw, unfiltered vision, were displayed like trophies. A wave of profound sadness washed over me, heavy and suffocating. I felt a strange urge to simply dissolve, to vanish into the shadows, to erase the evidence of my vulnerability. Someone, somewhere, had taken pieces of my inner landscape and placed them on public display without a word. I rubbed the bridge of my nose, a slow, weary gesture, wishing desperately for the simple anonymity of the dark.
